name: Tintic Prince Mine
short_description: Historic mining area in the Tintic mining district offering opportunities to find gold, silver, and quartz specimens on accessible BLM land near Eureka, Utah.
description: The Tintic Prince Mine is located within the historic Tintic mining district, one of Utah's most productive precious metal regions. This BLM-managed site sits at the heart of mineralized terrain that has yielded significant quantities of gold, silver, and associated minerals since the late 1800s. The area offers rockhounders a chance to explore legacy mining locations and search for quartz veins, fluorite specimens, and small gold flakes in nearby washes and hillsides. The terrain is moderately rocky with scattered mine dumps and exposed outcrops typical of historic hard-rock mining operations. Visitors should expect to see weathered quartz, iron-stained rock, and occasional fluorite crystals in the surrounding area. The combination of accessible BLM land, rich mineralogy, and historical significance makes this an excellent destination for intermediate rockhounders interested in precious metals and historic mining landscapes. Spring and fall offer the most comfortable collecting conditions.
history: The Tintic mining district boomed in the 1890s-1920s when miners extracted millions of dollars in gold and silver from fissure veins running through Precambrian and Paleozoic strata. The Tintic Prince Mine was one of several productive operations in this district, where hydrothermal fluids deposited precious metals along fractures in limestone and quartzite, creating the quartz-sulfide veins that rockhounders can still observe today.
tips: Focus your search on old mine dumps and the quartz-rich outcrops surrounding the mine portal area; these areas often yield small specimens of quartz, fluorite, and occasional gold flakes. Bring a geologist's hammer or small chisel to carefully break open quartz specimens and expose fresh surfaces where gold and fluorite crystals are most visible. Look for iron-oxide staining (rust-colored zones) on rock faces, as these indicate the presence of sulfide minerals that hosted the precious metals. Early morning searches are productive as afternoon heat can be intense; always carry extra water and sunscreen.
terrain_notes: Elevation approximately 6,500 feet. Rugged, rocky terrain with scattered sagebrush and juniper. The area features moderate slopes with exposed bedrock outcrops and weathered mine tailings. Walking distance from parking to prime collecting areas typically ranges from 0.25 to 1 mile. Footing is uneven and rocky; sturdy boots are essential.
best_season: April - May, September - October. These shoulder months provide moderate temperatures ideal for field work. Summer heat (June-August) can exceed 85 degrees Fahrenheit; winter snow (November-March) may restrict access to higher elevations.
written_directions: From Eureka, Utah, take US 50 northeast for approximately 3 miles toward the Tintic mining district. Look for BLM access roads and signs indicating the Tintic area. Turn onto Forest Road 015 (or local BLM roads marked for mineral collection). Continue for 2-4 miles on well-maintained dirt road until you reach the mine area and parking zones near the Tintic Prince Mine site. GPS coordinates (39.998209, -112.179129) will guide you to the specific location.
road_conditions: Dirt road to site, generally passable for passenger vehicles in dry conditions. May require high-clearance or AWD after heavy rain or winter weather.
parking_notes: Informal parking areas near mine site. Space for 3-5 vehicles at pullouts along access roads. No designated parking lot.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. Respect all mine safety barriers and do not enter unstable mine structures. Pack out all trash and minimize site disturbance.
rules:
Personal use only - no commercial collection
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No motorized excavation or heavy equipment
Do not enter mine structures or cross safety barriers
Pack out all trash
Minimize site disturbance
nearest_services: Eureka, Utah, approximately 3 miles west. Gas, food, and water available. Nearest hospital is in Spanish Fork, about 40 miles south.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with uneven, rocky footing requiring sturdy boots. No wheelchair access. Mild elevation gain and scattered rocks make this suitable for reasonably fit hikers age 8+. Not recommended for those with significant mobility limitations. Parking area is accessible by passenger vehicle on dry days.

faq.question: What gemstones and minerals can I find at Tintic Prince Mine in Utah?
faq.answer: You can find gold, silver, quartz specimens, and fluorite crystals at Tintic Prince Mine. The site is within the historic Tintic mining district where hydrothermal veins deposited these precious metals and minerals. Small gold flakes and quartz-fluorite specimens are commonly found in mine dumps and surrounding outcrops. Iron-stained quartz is particularly abundant in the area.

faq.question: Is there a fee to rockhound at Tintic Prince Mine, and do I need a permit?
faq.answer: No fee and no permit required. Tintic Prince Mine is located on BLM public land where recreational mineral collecting is allowed under FLPMA regulations at no cost. You are limited to 25 lbs per day for personal use only, with no commercial collection permitted.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to visit Tintic Prince Mine for rockhounding?
faq.answer: April-May and September-October are ideal. These shoulder seasons offer moderate temperatures (60-75 degrees Fahrenheit) perfect for field work. Summer heat can exceed 85 degrees and winter snow may restrict access. Spring and fall also provide the most comfortable conditions for exploring the rocky terrain.

faq.question: How difficult is rockhounding at Tintic Prince Mine for beginners?
faq.answer: Tintic Prince Mine is beginner-friendly. The terrain is moderately rocky but accessible to most hikers, and specimens are visible on surface and in mine dumps without requiring advanced skills. Children age 8+ can participate with adult supervision. The 25 lb daily limit and personal-use BLM rules make it straightforward for first-time rockhounders.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring to Tintic Prince Mine?
faq.answer: Bring a geologist's hammer or small chisel to break open quartz specimens and expose gold and fluorite crystals. Pack sturdy hiking boots, a backpack, extra water (at least 3 liters), sunscreen, and a hat. A 5-gallon bucket helps carry specimens up to your 25 lb daily limit. A magnifying glass helps identify minerals and gold flakes.

faq.question: How far is Tintic Prince Mine from Eureka, Utah, and what are driving directions?
faq.answer: Tintic Prince Mine is approximately 3 miles northeast of Eureka. From downtown Eureka, take US 50 northeast for 3 miles toward the Tintic mining district. Turn onto Forest Road 015 or marked BLM mineral collection roads and continue 2-4 miles on dirt road to the mine site. GPS coordinates 39.998209, -112.179129 will guide you directly there.

meta_description: Find gold, silver, and quartz at Tintic Prince Mine on Utah BLM land. No permit, 25 lb limit. Free rockhounding near Eureka. April-October best.
